Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place. I'd put in about 35 hours a week, but it wasn't organized in any real way. I'd grab time whenever I could—sometimes late at night, sometimes between other commitments. Without reliable internet at home, I had to plan around when I could access the campus library or a friend's place. A lot of my studying happened with physical notes and textbooks I'd borrowed or photocopied.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd be intensely focused on one subject, other weeks I'd barely touch anything because of extracurricular activities or just feeling overwhelmed. Deadlines definitely pushed me to cram, but I tried to spread things out when I could manage my anxiety about it all.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or needing to check if there were new announcements. Sometimes I'd log in just to download materials so I could study them offline later. Without consistent internet access, I had to be strategic about when I logged in—I'd try to grab everything I needed in one session.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. Using the LMS never became a natural habit for me because accessing the internet was always a bit of an event. I'd have to physically go somewhere with WiFi, so it felt more like a chore than a routine. The only pattern was logging in when I knew I absolutely had to download something or check grades.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely useful because I could download them and watch repeatedly on my phone offline. The discussion posts were also helpful because interacting with peers helped clarify concepts I was struggling with. I learned best by doing things hands-on, so anything I could practice or apply stuck with me better than just reading.
Q6: I often skipped the supplementary readings and some of the optional quizzes. With limited resources and time, I had to prioritize what would directly impact my grades. Some materials just felt redundant or too theoretical when I needed practical understanding. If it wasn't going to be assessed, it usually fell to the bottom of my list.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the exams or assignments. Also, anything that involved actually doing something—practicing problems, discussing with classmates—felt more valuable than passive reading. If I could feel myself understanding something better afterward, that made it worthwhile.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. With my anxiety levels and everything else going on, required work already stressed me out enough. Optional stuff felt nice in theory, but I'd always think about what else I should be doing instead. Required activities had clear consequences, so they always took priority.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs, lecture slides, and videos when I had internet access, then study them at home without any connectivity. I also used textbooks from the library and handwritten notes extensively. A lot of my learning happened through physically writing things out and practicing problems on paper.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours working through problems in my notebook or reviewing printed materials at home. None of that would show up anywhere in the LMS. I'd also study with friends in person, quizzing each other and explaining concepts—that was some of my most effective learning, but it left no digital trace.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access materials once, download everything I could, and then spend days working through them offline. My exam score reflects that—I was actually learning and understanding the material, just not in ways the system could track. One download might represent ten hours of offline study.
Q12: There were definitely periods where my anxiety spiked and I just couldn't focus on coursework. Competing responsibilities—extracurriculars, family stuff, financial pressures—would pile up and make it hard to engage. Sometimes I'd feel so overwhelmed that I'd avoid logging in altogether because even looking at pending tasks stressed me out.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or confused about something, the last thing I wanted to do was navigate the LMS. Competing responsibilities meant I had to triage constantly—sometimes coursework lost. The fatigue especially affected my assignment completion; I understood the material but couldn't always muster the energy to formally submit work.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere—worrying about other things or just too tired to concentrate. I'd technically be logged in and looking at content, but nothing was sticking. Those sessions felt like going through the motions.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments even when I knew they mattered. The anxiety made it worse—I'd avoid things because thinking about them stressed me out, which then made me more anxious about falling behind. It was a vicious cycle. Sometimes the avoidance was about not having the energy to do the work justice.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the main driver of my LMS usage. I'd log in more frequently as deadlines approached, trying to make sure I hadn't missed anything. The pressure helped me focus but also spiked my stress levels. Without deadlines, I probably would have engaged even less with the platform.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it motivated me to keep going and reassured me I was on the right track. Negative feedback sometimes made me want to avoid that type of task in the future. The response time for getting feedback affected whether I could actually use it—if it came too late, I'd already moved on mentally.
Q18: The course structure influenced me a lot. When things were clearly laid out with specific deadlines and requirements, I could plan around my limited internet access. But when the structure was loose or expectations were unclear, I'd feel lost and disengage. I needed clarity to manage my anxiety and competing demands.
Q19: Having downloadable materials made engagement much easier for someone like me without reliable internet. What made it harder was when activities required constant online presence or real-time participation. Discussion forums were okay because I could contribute asynchronously, but anything synchronous was nearly impossible for me to attend consistently.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying to understand the material but struggling to complete formal assignments. Maybe they're dealing with anxiety or perfectionism—they engage with content but can't bring themselves to submit work. Or they might be exploring and learning without feeling ready to commit to submissions.
Q21: That's pretty much my experience. My exam score was really high even though my assignment completion was low and my LMS activity was moderate. I was learning effectively offline—through downloaded materials, textbooks, and study groups. The LMS just couldn't capture where my real learning was happening.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access and completion of online tasks, which is useful for identifying who's completely absent. But they miss offline learning entirely, and they can't measure understanding or engagement quality. Someone could be logged in and completely zoned out, or logged off and deeply engaged with printed materials.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through motions to tick boxes. It's when I'm curious about a topic and want to master it, when I'm actively thinking and questioning rather than passively consuming. For me, engagement is internal—it's about mental investment, not just physical presence on a platform.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my extensive offline studying, my peer study sessions, or the mindfulness practices I used to manage my anxiety so I could actually focus. It wouldn't capture my genuine understanding of the material that led to my high exam score. It also wouldn't show the personal struggles I navigated while still managing to learn—the resilience that got me through despite limited resources and high stress.
